coredata.dc:title: Rapid detection of nitrite in food by multi-colorimetric and fluorescence dual-mode based on crystal violet modulated carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Yushen
Qiao, Wenteng
Fan, Xiaotong
Wang, Luliang
Wang, Mengjiao
Liu, Xinhui
Jia, Yuhan
Liu, Daotan
Liu, Tong
Zang, Mujun
Tian, Shengjun
Yin, Feng
coredata.dc:description: A new method for the determination of nitrite (NO2 –) by multicolor colorimetry and fluorescence dual-mode is presented. This sensing system involves the reaction of crystal violet modulated carbon dots (CV-CDs) with different concentrations of nitrite to produce multiple color signals, while the fluorescence intensity of CV-CDs increases with the increase of nitrite concentration. Therefore, the absorption wavelength shift and fluorescence intensity change can be used for the quantitative determination of nitrite. Furthermore, we combine the multicolor colorimetric output with a smartphone platform to perform on-site determination of nitrite without the need for a spectrometer. Intelligent recognition software was designed to take pictures of color signals and analyze R, G, and B channels. Good results of nitrite analysis were obtained.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Nitrite
Multi-colorimetric
Fluorescence
Software identification

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthetic of crystal violet modulated carbon dots (CV-CDs)
Double-mode detection of nitrite based on CV-CDs
Intelligent recognition based on RGB software
Operation principle of the dual-mode detection sensor based on CV-CDs
Rapid detection of nitrite based on RGB intelligent recognition software
Quantitation of nitrite in food samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A series of characterization of CV-CDs. a: TEM images (inset: HRTEM image of CV-CDs); b: TEM–EDS elemental mapping showing of C, N, O; c: Excitation dependent emission spectra of CV-CDs; d: UV–vis absorption and emission spectra of the obtained CV-CDs (insets: photographs of the CV-CDs solutions under daylight (left) and UV light (right)).

Feasibility verification characterization. UV–vis spectra (a) and Fluorescence spectra (b) of CV-CDs with water and NO2 – solution (inset: corresponding photograph under daylight and UV light); TEM images of CV-CDs with water (c) and NO2 – solution (d).

Color changes (a) and fluorescent photos (b) of the system with different concentrations of NO2 –; c: The linear relationship between absorption peak shift (△λ) and NO2 – concentrations from 0 to 20 mg L−1; d: The linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and NO2 – concentrations from 0 to 20 mg L−1.

Selectivity and interference studies. Photographs (a) and fluorescent photos (b) of the proposed system in the presence of different analysts (1–14: NO2 –, CO3 2–, CH3COO–, HPO4 2−, SO4 2−, HCO3 –, Cl−, Mg2+, Cu2+, K+, Glucose, Citric acid, Glutathione and L-Cysteine); The peak shift (△λ) (c) and Fluorescence intensity (d) of CV-CDs in response to different analytes. Error bars represent the standard deviation of three replicates.
Illustration of the mechanism of dual-mode induction of nitrite colorimetric fluorescence by CV-CDs.

The recoveries and RSD values of detecting NO2 – in food samples （
x
¯
± s
, n = 3）.

head.ce:title: Rapid detection of nitrite in food by multi-colorimetric and fluorescence dual-mode based on crystal violet modulated carbon dots

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: School of Food Engineering, Ludong University, Yantai, Shandong 264025, China (Y. Liu).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A new method for the determination of nitrite (NO2 –) by multicolor colorimetry and fluorescence dual-mode is presented. This sensing system involves the reaction of crystal violet modulated carbon dots (CV-CDs) with different concentrations of nitrite to produce multiple color signals, while the fluorescence intensity of CV-CDs increases with the increase of nitrite concentration. Therefore, the absorption wavelength shift and fluorescence intensity change can be used for the quantitative determination of nitrite. Furthermore, we combine the multicolor colorimetric output with a smartphone platform to perform on-site determination of nitrite without the need for a spectrometer. Intelligent recognition software was designed to take pictures of color signals and analyze R, G, and B channels. Good results of nitrite analysis were obtained.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nitrite (NO2 –) is the most common nitrogen-containing compound in nature and plays a vital role in a variety of physiological processes[1]. It is necessary to consume an appropriate range of nitrite to maintain physical health. Due to its ability to inhibit the growth of spoilage bacteria, enhance the distinctive color of food, and improve food flavor, nitrite is commonly used as a food additive and preservative in the food industry[2,3]. However, excessive and continuous intake of nitrites can impair the blood oxygen transport capacity of humans and animals, resulting in methemoglobinemia[4,5]. Severe cases can lead to death, which is particularly dangerous for pregnant women and babies under three months old[6,7]. Owing to the significant negative impact of nitrites on food safety, careful and regular monitoring of nitrites is receiving widespread attention. The development of timely and sensitive methods to accurately detect nitrite has attracted more and more attention. To achieve qualitative and quantitative detection of nitrite, numerous methods have been developed, including spectrophotometry[8,9], electrochemistry[10,11], chemiluminescence[12,13], fluorescence[14,15] and chromatography[16,17]. However, the above method has some problems such as low sensitivity, time-consuming, cumbersome operation and toxic reagent. Because of its wide distribution, achieving sensitive and accurate determination of trace nitrite remains challenging.

Compared with these methods, colorimetric detection of nitrite has been widely favored due to its advantages of simple operation and fast response. In recent years, precious metal nanoparticles have been used as sensitive, selective, and colored substrates for designing colorimetric sensors due to their extremely high extinction coefficient under visible light. For example, gold nanoparticles (AuNPs), gold nanorods (AuNRs), and sliver nanoparticles (AgNPs)[18–20], etc. Recently, research has found that carbon dots (CDs) have become good candidates for optical sensing due to their excellent luminescent properties, outstanding photostability, good biosafety, and simple preparation[21,22], making them a good candidate for optical sensing. Previously, Hu et al. used carbon points (C-dots) as fluorescent probes and neutral red as chromogenic agents to establish a fluorescence colorimetric dual-mode system for the detection of nitrite [3]. The study has inspired us, as this work introduces smartphones as detection tools, providing enhanced color recognition capabilities in comparison to traditional colorimetric methods. Compared with some colorimetric or fluorescent single-mode optical counterparts, fluorescence and colorimetric dual-function sensors not only provide highly sensitive fluorescence methods, but also are convenient for visual detection, making the analysis assay more efficient and handier [23,24]. Hence, the strategy of fluorescence and colorimetric analysis based on dual-mode sensing of nitrite is highly advisable. Subsequently, more and more CDs were developed for fluorescence and colorimetric dual-mode nitrite detection[25–27]. Aroused by the above-mentioned reports, we attempt to develop a simple and convenient preparation of CDs-based colorimetric fluorescence dual-mode probe for the determination of nitrite in food.

With the boom of smart phone hardware and software, smartphones have become very prevalent. Recent research suggests the use of smartphones as signal grabbers for colorimetric and fluorescence measurements. This approach not only reduces time and expense costs, but also supports on-demand analysis and on-site testing. Combined with smartphones, image-based and spectroscope-based approaches are currently being used to study a wide range of molecular properties in the chemical and biological sciences for biosensing and diagnostics[28–30].

Here, we proposed to build a multi-color colorimetric and fluorescence dual-mode sensor based on crystal violet debugging CDs (CV-CDs) for detecting nitrite content in food. The preparatory process of dual-mode detection sensors requires two procedures (Scheme 1 ). Firstly, water-soluble CV-CDs were synthesized according to the synthesis method reported in the literature. Then, the prepared CV-CDs were mixed with nitrite of different concentrations, and the degree of reaction between CV-CDs and nitrite was different to obtain a multicolor colorimetric sensor. In this system, at the excitation wavelength of 425 nm, it was shown that the green fluorescence intensity of the test tube gradually increases with the increase of nitrite concentration. Color images with different nitrite concentrations were collected by smartphones, and these images were recognized by self-developed software. At the same time, R, G, and B channel values were calculated and converted into corresponding nitrite concentrations. Thus, achieving the goal of the rapid quantitative analysis of nitrite concentration.
For detailed chemicals and instruments, please refer to Electronic Support Information (ESI).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthetic of crystal violet modulated carbon dots (CV-CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: An improved hydrothermal method was used for the synthesis of CV-CDs[25]. Briefly, crystal violet (10 mg) and citric acid (6 g) were dissolved in 20 mL ultrapure water by ultrasonic until completely dissolved. The dissolved solution was poured into the Teflon-lined autoclave at 200 °C for 4 h. Then cooled to room temperature. The resulting solution was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 15 min and further filtered with 0.22 μM filter element. The ultimate product is collected in a sample bottle and stored at 4 °C for later use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Double-mode detection of nitrite based on CV-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimal conditions, nitrite of different concentrations was mixed with CV-CDs solution. The final volume of the above solution was set at 0.6 mL. After 7 min incubation, the fluorescence emission (λex = 425 nm) and UV–vis absorption spectrum were collected for each. The selectivity of CV-CDs to nitrite was investigated by adding a variety of other anions or amino acids instead of nitrite in the same procedure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Intelligent recognition based on RGB software
ce:section.ce:para: Follow the same steps to detect different concentrations of NO2 –, and after the reaction was over, put the mixed solution in the round hole, using a mobile phone to collect photos. Finally, leveraging the underlying technology involves converting image metadata into R, G, and B channels, each represented by a two-dimensional matrix. The histogram of each two-dimensional matrix can be calculated using statistical methods. After obtaining the RGB channel histogram of the target image, normalization was applied. The normalized histogram was then compared with the histogram data of the standardized color reference card. The Bhattacharyya distance of the three-color channels was calculated and averaged to obtain the concentration corresponding to the color with the least error compared with the standardized color reference card.

The CV-CDs used in this experiment were obtained by a one-step hydrothermal method. The size and morphology of CV-CDs were observed by TEM and HRTEM (Fig. 1 a). The images display that CV-CDs have outstanding nanostructure and good dispersion with no obvious aggregation. The HRTEM image (insert Fig. 1a) shows that CV-CDs have a crystal structure with an interlayer lattice spacing of 0.22 nm, which matches the report[31]. According to the histogram of the size distribution of 100 particles selected in the TEM image, the diameter of CV-CDs ranges from 2 to 4 nm, with an average value of 3.15 nm (Fig. S1a). In addition, the surface properties of CV-CDs were further analyzed by TEM element mapping, FTIR spectra and Zeta potential measurement. The distribution of the three elements C, N and O in the CV-CDs spherical structure was shown in Fig. 1b. As illustrated in Fig. S1b, the broad absorption band appearing around 1213 cm−1, 1428 cm−1 and 1631 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of the C-O-C, C-N and C = C. The peaks at 1703 cm−1, 3298 cm−1 and 3493 cm−1 indicate the existence of C = O, N-H and O-H[25]. The Zeta potential measurement result was + 39.19 mv, which hints the surface of CV-CDs contains amino groups.

Moreover, we investigated the optical properties of CV-CDs by the Fluorescence and UV–vis absorption. Fig. 1c displayed a series of CV-CDs emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths, and the results indicated that the excitation at 425 nm was the optimal excitation for subsequent experiments. As demonstrated in Fig. 1d, a clear blue-purple solution can be observed under sunlight, indicating that CV-CDs have good dispersibility in water. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of CV-CDs showed a strong absorption band at 575 nm, that the reported spectrum in the wavelength range of 500–600 nm is similar to the UV–vis spectrum of crystal violet[32]. The emission spectrum of CV-CDs exhibited a broadband with a maximum of 495 nm upon excitation at 425 nm. Correspondingly, a bright fluorescence emission (inset of Fig. 1d) was observed under UV light (λex = 365 nm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Operation principle of the dual-mode detection sensor based on CV-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Here, the feasibility of a dual-mode detection sensor based on CV-CDs was confirmed by photography, UV–vis spectroscopy, TEM and Zeta potential studies. After the introduction of NO2 –, the interaction of NO2 – with the amino groups which on the surface of CV-CDs cause colorimetric and fluorescent color changes. As demonstrated in Fig. 2 a, after the addition of NO2 –, the absorbance of the positive group was significantly reduced and red-shifted, with the color of the solution changing from blue-purple to yellow (Fig. 2a inset). The fluorescence intensity of the positive group was significantly enhanced with the introduction of NO2 – into CV-CDs system. Interestingly, the fluorescence of the CV-CDs solution changes from blue-green to green (Fig. 2b inset). The obvious color changes of the two groups of solutions under daylight and fluorescence indicate that the detection method can be easily identified by the naked eye. The corresponding TEM images of CV-CDs were also tested during the reaction. As displayed in Fig. 2c and d, the initial CV-CDs were evenly dispersed with a size of about 3 nm. After mixing with NO2 –, the TEM images showed that the crystals were aggregated and the size increased.

Besides, Crystal violet, CV-CDs, nitrite, and CV-CDs+ nitrite mixtures were measured by a Zeta potential measurement (Fig. S2). It can be observed that Crystal violet and nitrite in aqueous solution were +25.54 mV and −26.41 mV, respectively, while the surface charge of CV-CDs+ nitrite mixture was +3.78 mV. It was speculated that the reaction between CV-CDs and NO2 – caused the aggregation of nanoparticles, which was similar to aggregation-induced luminescence, so the fluorescence intensity was enhanced[33,34]. The above results demonstrated that the sensor system can be applied to the dual-mode detection of NO2 –.

To obtain the optimal signal answer, a range of factors need to be studied, such as CV-CDs concentration, reaction time, incubation temperature and pH value are not negligible. The aforementioned experimental parameters were optimized to obtain the optimal detection results.

In line with the principle of the dual-mode assay, the concentration of CV-CDs had the most significant impact on the system. To achieve the best effect and economical purpose, the reaction intensity of CV-CDs and nitrite at different concentrations was determined by UV–vis absorption spectrum and fluorescence spectra respectively. Since the size of CV-CDs reaction to nitrite changes and aggregates were formed, the data were processed with the UV–vis absorption peak shift (△λ) and the fluorescence intensity values. It can be observed from Fig. S3a that as the concentration of CV-CDs increased, the peak shift △λ and the fluorescence intensity gradually increased sequentially. When the concentration was 0.11 mg mL−1, the peak shift △λ and fluorescence intensity increase to the maximum, and then slightly decrease. Therefore, the concentration of CV-CDs used in subsequent experiments was determined to be 0.11 mg mL−1. Additionally, since the instability of nitrite, a faster method for on-site analysis is essential, and the incubation time was optimized to save time. The results showed that with the extension of incubation time, both the peak shift △λ and fluorescence intensity gradually increased and reached the maximum at 7 min. Therefore, the subsequent incubation time was set to 7 min (Fig. S3b). In the following temperature optimization, we found that the maximum displacement was achieved in 25 ℃, while for fluorescence intensity, there was little difference between 15 ℃ and 25 ℃. After comprehensive consideration, we decided to conduct future experiments under room temperature conditions (Fig. S3c). Finally, we optimized the pH in the experimental conditions, and the data results showed that there was no significant change in △λ or fluorescence intensity under different pH conditions. Considering the preparation factors of the experimental conditions, the final pH for subsequent experiments was determined to be 7(Fig. S3d).

Through the optimized conditions discussed above, the UV–vis absorbance and fluorescence responses of the CV-CDs system to different concentrations of NO2 – were investigated to reflect the sensitivity of the system. As shown in Fig. 3 a, with the increase of NO2 – concentration, the color of CV-CDs solution turns to purple, blue-purple, green, yellow-green and yellow successively. When the concentration of NO2 – was 5 mg L−1, the color of the positive tube was significantly different from that of the negative tube, and then the color difference was even worse. Therefore, the naked eye detection limit of the detection system was determined to be 5 mg L−1. At the same time, the peak shift △λ gradually decreased and was accompanied by a red-shift phenomenon (Fig. S4a). This change in displacement was attributed to different concentrations of NO2 – which may interact with an amino group to form a diazotized substance. In particular, when NO2 – concentration was in the range of 0–20 mg L−1, the nitrite concentration showed a good linear correlation with the absorbance ratio (Fig. 3c). The corresponding linear regression equation is Y = 1.5562X + 1.4025 (R2 = 0.9767), where X is the concentration of NO2 –. The detection limit for NO2 – was calculated to be 0.05 mg L−1 according to the rule of 3δ/S.

Next, the fluorescence characteristics of CV-CDs in the presence of NO2 – at different concentrations were studied. It can be seen from Fig. 3b that with the increase of NO2 – content, the fluorescence gradually changes from blue-green to green, accompanied by the increase in fluorescence intensity. With the same naked-eye judgment method, the detection limit of fluorescence was determined to be 5 mg L−1. The spectrogram of Fig. S4b further verifies the change in fluorescence characteristics of CV-CDs. The statistical fluorescence intensity revealed an excellent linear relationship with the fluorescence intensity in the range of 0–20 mg L−1 (Fig. 3d). The corresponding linear regression equation is Y = 3439.9X + 239002 (R2 = 0.9966), where X is the concentration of NO2 –. The detection limit for NO2 – was calculated to be 0.6 mg L−1 according to the rule of 3δ/S.

The above results indicate that the CV-CDs system is suitable for dual-mode nitrite detection. The comparison between the proposed dual-mode system and other analytical methods for detecting NO2 – is shown in Table S1. The detection limit is as low as 0.05 mg L−1, which is far below Chinese National standards for food safety and limits set by the World Health Organization (WHO) and European Scientific Committee on Food (SCF)[35 36]. It implies that our method can meet the needs of rapid food detection and screening. On the one hand, the rich color variation of the system can be used for semi-quantitative detection of NO2 – by the naked eye, on the other hand, it has the function of accurate quantitative detection of laboratory instruments, and multi-channel detection further ensures the accuracy of the results, while saving time and operation steps.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Rapid detection of nitrite based on RGB intelligent recognition software
ce:section.ce:para: In addition, this study developed fast and efficient analysis software to meet the needs of rapid colorimetric quantitative analysis. This approach serves as a purely visual detection solution that does not rely on fluorescence spectrometers or UV–vis spectrometers to detect experimental results. The smartphone-based measurement method steps are mentioned in the Supporting Material. As displayed in Fig. S5, the underlying technology involves the conversion of image metadata into R, G, and B channels, with the X-axis representing the range of color intensity values and the Y-axis representing the frequency or count of data within the corresponding value range. After obtaining the R, G and B channel histogram of the target image, the normalization process was carried out. The average Bhattacharya distance of each channel is calculated to obtain the corresponding concentration value. According to the running program video, the concentration value of the object in the experimental hole can be accurately identified. Several groups of experimental photos under different light are selected for testing, and the results show that the consistency rate was 100.00 %(ESI2-Video). The results suggest that the software can accurately identify experimental photos, laying a foundation for simple and rapid laboratory detection of objects. Combined with the intelligent software recognition system developed by smart phones as a signal detection tool, compared with the signal output method of other color comparison methods, smart phones can capture and detect signals more conveniently and quickly. By using the accurate and rapid batch image processing function of intelligent software, this system can get rid of the dependence of instruments for signal recognition and realize rapid detection and diversified color detection. At the same time, the accuracy and reliability of the results are guaranteed.

To assess the selectivity of CV-CDs for NO2 –, the peak shift △λ and the fluorescence intensity to the interferences were investigated at a concentration of 20 mg L−1. The concentration of interfering substances was 100 mg L−1 in the process. The experimental results were shown in Fig. 4 , including NO3 –, CO3 2–, CH3COO–, HPO4 2−, SO4 2−, HCO3 –, Cl−, Mg2+, Cu2+, K+, Glucose, Citric acid, Glutathione and L-Cysteine. It can be observed that only NO2 – causes color changes in the CV-CDs of the detection system, accompanied by peak shift △λ changes. Fluorescence performance studies have found that the introduction of nitrite significantly enhances the fluorescence of the test tube. However, when the concentration of these interfering substances reaches 5 times the concentration of NO2 –, there was no significant change in their fluorescence intensity. The results indicate that the detection system has a good accurate analytical ability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitation of nitrite in food samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to explore the analytical feasibility of this detection strategy in the field of environmental detection and food safety, considering the diversity of food samples, the dual-mode detection strategy was applied to detect NO2 – in several familiar real food samples, including pure water, Fuling mustard and sausage. These real food samples were pretreated as mentioned in the supporting materials. The standard addition method was used for experimental evaluation. It can be seen from Table 1 that the recovery rate of food standard addition is 85.27–112.30 %, and the relative standard deviation is less than 12.00 %. Furthermore, two commercial test kits were used to detect 10 mg L−1 of NO2 – samples and compared with our method (Fig. S6). The results also show that the method has good reliability and feasibility. Notably, the entire detection time was completed within 10 min, eliminating complex operational steps. These results indicated that the proposed method has potential application prospects for real-time and on-site nitrite detection in different foods.

To sum up, a software-assisted mobile phone combined with an optical device multi-channel output detection signal for colorimetric fluorescence dual-mode monitoring of NO2 – in food. Using CV-CDs as the response signal, mobile phone, ultraviolet spectrophotometer and fluorescence spectrophotometer were respectively used to collect signals, and a more accurate linear relationship between polychromatic signal and fluorescence signal and the concentration of the detected substance was established to realize visual semi-quantitative detection. It can obtain more accurate and sensitive detection results, provide an effective solution for food contamination problems, and further provide unlimited potential for food safety detection. It is worth noting that the design and development of the underlying technology to draw the RGB channel histogram of mobile phone pictures, convert the average Bhattacharya distance of each channel into the corresponding concentration value. Based on the combination of RGB value analysis software and multi-color colorimetric sensor, the visual nano sensor constructed can intelligently detect nitrite content in food.

Yushen Liu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Wenteng Qiao: Methodology, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Xiaotong Fan: Methodology, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Luliang Wang: Methodology, Funding acquisition. Mengjiao Wang: Formal analysis. Xinhui Liu: Formal analysis. Yuhan Jia: Methodology. Daotan Liu: Software. Tong Liu: Software. Mujun Zang: Software. Shengjun Tian: Software. Feng Yin: Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by the Chinese National Natural Science Foundation (Grant No. 82003502 and 21904053), the Science and Technology Plan Project of Yantai (Grant No. 2022XDRH004), the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (Grant No. ZR2023MB107) and Shandong Key Laboratory of Biochemical Analysis (SKLBA2307).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.110334.
